rule.desc: Mark properties, parameters, and arrays as `readonly` by default. Remove `readonly` only when mutation is intentional.
rule.rationale: Readonly annotations catch accidental mutation at compile time and communicate intent clearly.

rule.desc: Use `as const` for literal objects and arrays that should not change.
rule.rationale: Const assertions make all properties `readonly` and narrow types to their literal values, enabling type-level computation.

rule.desc: Prefer pure functions: same input produces same output, no side effects.
rule.rationale: Pure functions are easier to test, reason about, compose, and parallelize.

rule.desc: Return new objects or arrays instead of mutating inputs. Use spread syntax or utility methods.
rule.rationale: Returning new values prevents shared-state bugs, makes data flow explicit, and works well with React, Redux, and other immutable-data architectures.

rule.desc: Prefer `map`, `filter`, `reduce`, and other array methods over imperative loops for data transformations.
rule.rationale: Array methods express intent clearly, chain naturally, and avoid off-by-one errors. Use `for...of` when performance matters in hot paths or when early termination is needed.

rule.desc: Avoid module-level mutable state. Use dependency injection or factory functions instead.
rule.rationale: Module-level mutable state creates hidden coupling, makes testing difficult, and causes surprising behavior in concurrent environments.
